metadata.title: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum / wave / pattern; CRIMSON = single dot / localized detection
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Bragg/Davisson-Germer angle calc, no which-path/decoherence, no biprism optics.

beats.narration_text: Send electrons through a double slit one at a time — never two in the machine at once. An interference pattern still assembles itself, dot by dot.

card.copy: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself

beats.narration_text: Water waves through two slits: classic interference. The two wavefronts overlap and add up — bright where they reinforce, dark where they cancel.
Two waves need two things in the machine at once. If there's only one electron at a time — nothing to interfere with — classical physics predicts a smooth, featureless smear. Not fringes.

card.copy: One electron at a time: no partner to interfere with. Classical predicts a smear. The experiment shows fringes. Why?

beats.narration_text: Each electron lands as a single dot — a click, a spot, one definite place. That part is perfectly particle-like.
But the wave function of that single electron passed through both slits. It spread out from each slit and interfered with itself on the far side.
Where the two wavefronts reinforce, the probability density is high. Where they cancel, the probability is nearly zero. The dot is most likely to land on a bright fringe.
As electrons accumulate — ten, then thousands — the dot pattern fills in. The histogram of where they land matches the wave interference pattern exactly.
This is the de Broglie wavelength in action: lambda equals h over p. The electron's momentum determines the fringe spacing, just as a wavelength would.
Illustrative example: electrons at 54 eV have a de Broglie wavelength of about 0.17 nanometers — comparable to atomic spacing. That wavelength sets the fringe spacing you'd measure.
The electron lands in one spot. Its wave function traveled both paths. The probability of each spot comes from the wave — and the wave is the whole electron, all of it, all at once.

card.copy: One electron. Both slits. One dot. The wave was the whole electron.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
